title: Two weeks to go and country still waits for details on 'new' super tax
full_text:
More than three years after announcing a new tax on high-value superannuation accounts, and two weeks before it comes into effect, the government has yet to explain how the impost will apply to those on defined benefit pensions, including scores of retired MPs.
Frustrated financial advisers seeking answers for clients, be they former politicians, public servants or defence force personnel, are being told by the Commonwealth Superannuation Corporation, which manages public defined benefit accounts, that it is also in the dark.
Recent emails seen by The Australian Financial Review, exchanged between the CSC and financial advisers seeking information, express frustration at what the CSC describes as radio silence from the Treasury and the Australian Taxation Office.
The Self-Managed Superannuation Fund Association said it had been advised that the application of the Division 296 tax to defined benefits would be outlined by regulations that would be finalised by July 1, but it had no further information. It also held concerns about the application of the tax to death benefits.
Late on Tuesday, a spokesperson for Financial Services Minister Daniel Mulino said the release of the final regulations was imminent and that there was no urgency because the first liabilities would not be assessed until the end of the next financial year.
Treasurer Jim Chalmers announced in February 2023 that earnings on the component of superannuation funds above $3 million would be subject to an additional 15 per cent tax. Initially, the $3 million was not to be indexed, and the tax would also apply to unrealised gains. Chalmers was forced to abandon both these measures last year following a public and internal backlash.
At the time the Division 296 tax was announced, the government promised that it would calculate a method by which defined benefits would be similarly taxed, given the pensions being disbursed from such funds are typically equivalent to the earnings from large superannuation funds.
"It is the government's intent to ensure broadly commensurate treatment for defined benefit interests," Chalmers said in a note released at the time. "Treasury will consult further on the appropriate treatment for defined benefit interests."
Although there are only a few pre-2004 politicians left in parliament, hundreds of retired MPs are on the old scheme, not to mention multitudes of retired senior public servants and defence force personnel, all on large six-figure pensions.
"The government has consistent form pursuing tax increases first, then implementation after," Shadow treasurer Tim Wilson said. "It leaves small businesses and investors in their new taxes wake and undermines certainty and Australia's future prosperity."
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Phillip Coorey
heading: Superannuation and Investments
